audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman telephoning for information about a festival.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Stretton Festival box office.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: My family and I are on holiday in the area, and we've seen some posters about the festival this week.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Could you tell me about some of the events, please?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Uh. First of all, are there still tickets available for the jazz band on Saturday?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: There are, but only £15.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The £12 seats have all been sold.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The man says that tickets are available for 15 pounds, so 15 has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 7.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Stretton Festival box office.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Oh, hello.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: My family and I are on holiday in the area, and we've seen some posters about the festival this week.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Could you tell me about some of the events, please?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Uh. First of all, are there still tickets available for the jazz band on Saturday?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: There are, but only £15.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The £12 seats have all been sold.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: OK, and the venue is the school, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The secondary school.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Make sure you don't go to the primary school by mistake.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And there's an additional performer who isn't mentioned on the posters.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Carolyn Hart is going to play with the band.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Oh, I think I've heard her on the radio.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Doesn't she play the oboe or flute or something?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Yes, the flute.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: She usually plays with symphony orchestras, and apparently this is her first time with a jazz band.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Well, I'd certainly like to hear her.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Then the next thing I want to ask about is the duck races.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I saw a poster beside a river.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: What are they exactly?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Well, you buy a yellow plastic duck, or as many as you like.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: They're a pound each, and you write your name on each one.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: There'll be several races, depending on the number of ducks taking part.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And John Stevens, a champion swimmer who lives locally, is going to start the races.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: All the ducks will be launched into the river at the back of the cinema.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Then they'll float along the river for 500 meters, as far as the railway bridge.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And are there any prizes?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Yes, the first duck in each race to arrive at the finishing line wins its owner free tickets for the concert on the last night of the festival.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: You said you can buy a duck.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I'm sure my children will both want one.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: They're on sale at a stall in the market.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: You can't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: It's got an enormous sign showing a couple of ducks.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: OK, I'll go there this afternoon.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I remember walking past there yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Now, uh, could you tell me something about the flower show, please?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Well, admission is free.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: And the show is being held in Bythwaite Hall.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Sorry, how do you spell that?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: BYTHWAITE.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Bythwaite.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Is it easy to find?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I'm not very familiar with the town yet.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Oh, you won't have any problem.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: It's right in the center of Stretton.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: It's the only old building in the town, so it's easy to recognize.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: I know it.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I presume it's open all day.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Yes, but if you'd like to see the prizes being awarded for the best flowers, you'll need to be there at 5 o'clock.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: The prizes are being given by a famous actor.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Kevin Shapeless, he lives nearby and gets involved in a lot of community events.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Gosh, I've seen him on TV.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I'll definitely go to the prize-giving.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 8 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've seen a list of plays that are being performed this week, and I'd like to know which are suitable for my children, and which ones my husband and I might go to.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: How old are your children?
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: 5 and 7. What about 'The Mystery of Muldoon'?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: That's aimed at 5 to 10 year olds.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: So, if I take my children, I can expect them to enjoy it more than I do.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I think so.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: If you'd like something for yourself and your husband and leave your children with a babysitter, you might like to see 'Fire and Flood'.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: It's about events that really happened in Stretton 200 years ago, and children might find it rather frightening.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: Oh, thanks for the warning.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: And finally, what about 'Silly Sailor'?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: That's a comedy, and it's for young and old.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: In fact, it won an award in the Stretton Drama Festival a couple of months ago.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: OK, well, goodbye, and thanks for all the information.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I'm looking forward to the festival.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: ROB

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Plays

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a guide at an art museum talking to a group of visitors.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, and welcome to the museum - one with a remarkable range of exhibits, which I'm sure you'll enjoy.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: My name is Greg, and I'll tell you about the various collections as we go around.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: But before we go, let me just give you a taste of what we have here.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Well, for one thing, we have a fine collection of 20th and 21st century paintings, many by very well-known artists.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: I'm sure you'll recognize several of the paintings.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: This is the gallery that attracts the largest number of visitors, so it's best to go in early in the day before the crowds arrive.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Then there are the 19th century paintings.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: The museum was opened in the middle of that century, and several of the artists each donated one work to get the museum started, as it were.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: So they're of special interest to us.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: We feel closer to them than to other works.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: The Sculpture Gallery has a number of fine exhibits, but I'm afraid it's currently closed for refurbishment.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: You'll need to come back next year to see it properly, but a number of the sculptures have been moved to other parts of the museum.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Around the world is a temporary exhibition.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: You've probably seen something about it on TV or in the newspapers.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: It's created a great deal of interest because it presents objects from every continent and many countries.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And provides information about their social context, why they were made, who for, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Then there's the collection of coins.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: This is what you might call a focused specialist collection.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Because all the coins come from this country and were produced between 2,000 and 1,000 years ago, and many of them were discovered by ordinary people digging their gardens and donated to the museum.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: All our porcelain and glass were left to the museum by its founder when he died in 1878, and in the terms of his will, we're not allowed to add anything to that collection.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: He believed it was perfect in itself, and we don't see any reason to disagree.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, that was something about the collections, and now here's some more practical information, in case you need it.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Most of the museum facilities are downstairs in the basement.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: So you go down the stairs here.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: When you reach the bottom of the stairs, you'll find yourself in a sitting area, with comfortable chairs and sofas, where you can have a rest before continuing your exploration of the museum.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: We have a very good restaurant which serves excellent food all day, in a relaxing atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: To reach it, when you get to the bottom of the stairs, go straight ahead.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: To the far side of the sitting area, then turn right into the corridor.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: You'll see the door of the restaurant facing you.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: If you just want a snack, or if you'd like to eat somewhere with facilities for children, we also have a cafe.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: When you reach the bottom of the stairs, you'll need to go straight ahead.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Turn right into the corridor, and the cafe is immediately on the right.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And talking about children, there are baby changing facilities downstairs.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: Cross the sitting area, continue straight ahead along the corridor on the left.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: And you and your baby will find the facilities on the left-hand side.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: The cloakroom where you should leave coats, umbrellas and any large bags is on the left-hand side of the sitting area.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: It's through the last door before you come to the corridor.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: There are toilets on every floor, but in the basement, they're the first rooms on the left when you get down there.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: OK, now if you've got anything to leave in the cloakroom, please do that now, and then we'll start our tour.
audioscript.speaker: GREG

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: You've probably seen something about it on TV or in the newspapers.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: It's created a great deal of interest because it presents objects from every continent and many countries.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: And provides information about their social context, why they were made, who for, and so on.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: Then there's the collection of coins.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: This is what you might call a focused specialist collection.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: Because all the coins come from this country and were produced between 2,000 and 1,000 years ago, and many of them were discovered by ordinary people digging their gardens and donated to the museum.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: All our porcelain and glass were left to the museum by its founder when he died in 1878, and in the terms of his will, we're not allowed to add anything to that collection.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: He believed it was perfect in itself, and we don't see any reason to disagree.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: OK, that was something about the collections, and now here's some more practical information, in case you need it.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: Most of the museum facilities are downstairs in the basement.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: So you go down the stairs here.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: When you reach the bottom of the stairs, you'll find yourself in a sitting area, with comfortable chairs and sofas, where you can have a rest before continuing your exploration of the museum.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: We have a very good restaurant which serves excellent food all day, in a relaxing atmosphere.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: To reach it, when you get to the bottom of the stairs, go straight ahead.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: To the far side of the sitting area, then turn right into the corridor.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: You'll see the door of the restaurant facing you.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: If you just want a snack, or if you'd like to eat somewhere with facilities for children, we also have a cafe.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: When you reach the bottom of the stairs, you'll need to go straight ahead.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: Turn right into the corridor, and the cafe is immediately on the right.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: And talking about children, there are baby changing facilities downstairs.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: Cross the sitting area, continue straight ahead along the corridor on the left.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: And you and your baby will find the facilities on the left-hand side.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: The cloakroom where you should leave coats, umbrellas and any large bags is on the left-hand side of the sitting area.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: It's through the last door before you come to the corridor.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: There are toilets on every floor, but in the basement, they're the first rooms on the left when you get down there.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: OK, now if you've got anything to leave in the cloakroom, please do that now, and then we'll start our tour.
utterances.speaker: GREG

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What does the speaker say about each of the following collections?
textReadable: What does the speaker say about each of the following collections?

list: was recently publicised in the media
includes some items given by members of the public
includes some items given by the artists
includes the most popular exhibits in the museum
is the largest of its kind in the country
has had some of its contents relocated
body.title: Collections

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a student called Joanna talking to her new supervisor about some research she has done on psychology and music.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Joanna.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Good to meet you.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Now, before we discuss your new research project, I'd like to hear something about the psychology study you did last year for your master's degree.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: So, uh, how did you choose your subjects for that?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Well, I had six subjects, all professional musicians, and all female.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Three were violinists, and there was also a cello player, and a pianist, and a flute player.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: They were all very highly regarded in the music world, and they'd done quite extensive tours in different continents.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: And quite a few had won prizes and competitions as well.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: And they were quite young, weren't they?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Yes, between 25 and 29.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Um. The mean was 27.8.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: I wasn't specifically looking for artists who'd produced recordings, but this is something that's just taken for granted these days, and they all had.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Now, you collected your data through telephone interviews, didn't you?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: I realized if I was going to interview leading musicians, it would only be possible over the phone, because they're so busy.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: I recorded them using a telephone recording adapter.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: I'd been worried about the quality, but it worked out all right.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: I managed at least a 30-minute interview with each subject, sometimes longer.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Did doing it on the phone make it more stressful?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: I'd thought it might...
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Um. It was all quite informal though, and in fact they seemed very keen to talk.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: I don't think using the phone meant I got less rich data, rather the opposite in fact.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Interesting.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: And you were looking at how performers dress for concert performances.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Uh. My research investigated the way players see their role as a musician, and how this is linked to the type of clothing they decide to wear.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: But that focus didn't emerge immediately.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Uh. When I started, I was more interested in trying to investigate the impact of what was worn on those listening, and also whether someone like a violinist might adopt a different style of clothing from say someone playing the flute or the trumpet.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: It's interesting that the choice of dress is up to the individual, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Yes, you'd expect there to be rules about it in orchestras, but that's quite rare.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You only had women performers in your study.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Was that because male musicians are less worried about fashion?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: I think a lot of the men are very much influenced by fashion.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: But in social terms, the choices they have are more limited.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: They'd really upset audiences if they strayed away from quite narrow boundaries.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Popular music has quite different expectations.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Uh. Did you read Mike Frost's article about the dress of women performers in popular music?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Well, he points out that a lot of female singers and musicians in popular music tend to dress down in performances.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: And wear less feminine clothes, um, like jeans instead of skirts.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Uh. And he suggests this is because otherwise they'd just be discounted as trivial.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: But you could argue they're just wearing what's practical.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: I mean a pop music concert is usually a pretty energetic affair.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Yes, uh, he doesn't make that point, but I think you're probably right.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: I was interested by the effect of the audience at a musical performance when it came to the choice of dress.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: The subjects I interviewed felt this was really important.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: It's all to do with what we understand by performance as a public event.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: They believed the audience had certain expectations.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: And it was up to them as performers to fulfill these expectations to show a kind of esteem.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: They weren't afraid of looking as if they'd made an effort to look good.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: I think in the past the audience would have had those expectations of one another too, but that's not really the case now, not in the UK anyway.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: And I also got interested in what sports scientists are doing too with regard to clothing.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Musicians are quite vulnerable physically, aren't they?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Because the movements they carry out are very intensive and repetitive.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: So, I'd imagine some features of sports clothing could safeguard the players from the potentially dangerous effects of this sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: Yes, but musicians don't really consider it.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: They avoid clothing that obviously restricts their movements, but that's as far as they go.
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: Anyway, coming back to your own research, do you have any idea where you're going from here?
audioscript.speaker: SUPERVISOR

audioscript.text: I was thinking of doing a study using an audience including...
audioscript.speaker: JOANNA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO characteristics were shared by the subjects of Joanna's psychology study?
options: They had all won prizes for their music.
They had all made music recordings.
They were all under 27 years old.
They had all toured internationally.
They all played a string instrument.

body.title: Which TWO points does Joanna make about her use of telephone interviews?
options: It meant rich data could be collected.
It allowed the involvement of top performers.
It led to a stressful atmosphere at times.
It meant interview times had to be limited.
It caused some technical problems.

body.title: Which TWO topics did Joanna originally intend to investigate in her research?
options: regulations concerning concert dress
audience reactions to the dress of performers
changes in performer attitudes to concert dress
how choice of dress relates to performer roles
links between musical instrument and dress choice

items.title: Joanna concentrated on women performers because
options: women are more influenced by fashion.
women's dress has led to more controversy.
women's code of dress is less strict than men's.

items.title: Mike Frost's article suggests that in popular music, women's dress is affected by
options: their wish to be taken seriously.
their tendency to copy each other.
their reaction to the masculine nature of the music.

items.title: What did Joanna's subjects say about the audience at a performance?
options: The musicians' choice of clothing is linked to respect for the audience.
The clothing should not distract the audience from the music.
The audience should make the effort to dress appropriately.

items.title: According to the speakers, musicians could learn from sports scientists about
options: the importance of clothing for physical freedom.
the part played by clothing in improving performance.
the way clothing may protect against physical injury.

test_text.title: Use of soil to reduce carbon dioxide in atmosphere

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture about a way of reducing the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As we saw in the last lecture, a major cause of climate change is the rapid rise in the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere over the last century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If we could reduce the amount of CO₂, perhaps the rate of climate change could also be slowed down.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One potential method involves enhancing the role of the soil that plants grow in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With regard to absorbing CO₂.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Rattan Lal, a soil scientist from Ohio State University in the USA, claims that the world's agricultural soils could potentially absorb 13% of the carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, the equivalent of the amount released in the last 30 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And research is going on into how this might be achieved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Lal first came to the idea that soil might be valuable in this way, not through an interest in climate change, but rather out of concern for the land itself, and the people dependent on it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Carbon rich soil is dark, crumbly and fertile, and retains some water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But erosion can occur if soil is dry, which is a likely effect.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If it contains inadequate amounts of carbon, erosion is of course bad for people trying to grow crops or breed animals on that terrain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the 1970s and '80s, Lal was studying soils in Africa, so devoid of organic matter that the ground had become extremely hard.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Like cement, there he met a pioneer in the study of global warming, who suggested that carbon from the soil had moved into the atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is now looking increasingly likely.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let me explain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For millions of years, carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere have been regulated in part by a natural partnership between plants and microbes, tiny organisms in the soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air, and transform it into sugars and other carbon-based substances.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While a proportion of these carbon products remain in the plant, some transfer from the roots to fungi and soil microbes, which store the carbon in the soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The invention of agriculture some 10,000 years ago disrupted these ancient soil-building processes and led to the loss of carbon from the soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When humans started draining the natural topsoil and plowing it up for planting, they exposed the buried carbon to oxygen.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This created carbon dioxide and released it into the air, and in some places, grazing by domesticated animals has removed all vegetation, releasing carbon into the air.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Tons of carbon have been stripped from the world's soils where it's needed, and pumped into the atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what can be done?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Researchers are now coming up with evidence that even modest changes to farming can significantly help to reduce the amount of carbon in the atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some growers have already started using an approach known as regenerative agriculture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This aims to boost the fertility of soil and keep it moist through established practices.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These include keeping fields planted all year round, and increasing the variety of plants being grown.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Strategies like these can significantly increase the amount of carbon stored in the soil, so agricultural researchers are now building a case for their use in combating climate change.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One American investigation into the potential for storing CO₂ on agricultural lands is taking place in California.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Soil scientist Whendee Silver of the University of California, Berkeley is conducting a first-of-its-kind study on a large cattle farm in the state.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: She and her students are testing the effects on carbon storage of the compost that is created from waste.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Both agricultural, including manure and cornstalks, and waste produced in gardens, such as leaves, branches, and lawn trimmings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Australia, soil ecologist Christine Jones is testing another promising soil enrichment strategy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Jones and 12 farmers are working to build up soil carbon by cultivating grasses that stay green all year round.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Like composting, the approach has already been proved experimentally.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Jones now hopes to show that it can be applied on working farms, and that the resulting carbon capture can be accurately measured.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's hoped in the future that projects such as these will demonstrate the role that farmers and other land managers can play in reducing the harmful effects of greenhouse gases.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, in countries like the United States, where most farming operations use large applications of fertilizer, changing such long standing habits will require a change of system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Rattan Lal argues that farmers should receive payment, not just for the corn or beef they produce, but also for the carbon they can store in their soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another study being carried out.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear part of a lecture about a way of reducing the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: As we saw in the last lecture, a major cause of climate change is the rapid rise in the level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere over the last century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If we could reduce the amount of CO₂, perhaps the rate of climate change could also be slowed down.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One potential method involves enhancing the role of the soil that plants grow in.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: With regard to absorbing CO₂.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Rattan Lal, a soil scientist from Ohio State University in the USA, claims that the world's agricultural soils could potentially absorb 13% of the carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, the equivalent of the amount released in the last 30 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And research is going on into how this might be achieved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Lal first came to the idea that soil might be valuable in this way, not through an interest in climate change, but rather out of concern for the land itself, and the people dependent on it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Carbon rich soil is dark, crumbly and fertile, and retains some water.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But erosion can occur if soil is dry, which is a likely effect.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If it contains inadequate amounts of carbon, erosion is of course bad for people trying to grow crops or breed animals on that terrain.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the 1970s and '80s, Lal was studying soils in Africa, so devoid of organic matter that the ground had become extremely hard.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Like cement, there he met a pioneer in the study of global warming, who suggested that carbon from the soil had moved into the atmosphere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is now looking increasingly likely.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let me explain.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For millions of years, carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere have been regulated in part by a natural partnership between plants and microbes, tiny organisms in the soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air, and transform it into sugars and other carbon-based substances.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: While a proportion of these carbon products remain in the plant, some transfer from the roots to fungi and soil microbes, which store the carbon in the soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The invention of agriculture some 10,000 years ago disrupted these ancient soil-building processes and led to the loss of carbon from the soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When humans started draining the natural topsoil and plowing it up for planting, they exposed the buried carbon to oxygen.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This created carbon dioxide and released it into the air, and in some places, grazing by domesticated animals has removed all vegetation, releasing carbon into the air.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Tons of carbon have been stripped from the world's soils where it's needed, and pumped into the atmosphere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what can be done?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Researchers are now coming up with evidence that even modest changes to farming can significantly help to reduce the amount of carbon in the atmosphere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some growers have already started using an approach known as regenerative agriculture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This aims to boost the fertility of soil and keep it moist through established practices.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These include keeping fields planted all year round, and increasing the variety of plants being grown.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Strategies like these can significantly increase the amount of carbon stored in the soil, so agricultural researchers are now building a case for their use in combating climate change.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One American investigation into the potential for storing CO₂ on agricultural lands is taking place in California.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Soil scientist Whendee Silver of the University of California, Berkeley is conducting a first-of-its-kind study on a large cattle farm in the state.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: She and her students are testing the effects on carbon storage of the compost that is created from waste.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Both agricultural, including manure and cornstalks, and waste produced in gardens, such as leaves, branches, and lawn trimmings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Australia, soil ecologist Christine Jones is testing another promising soil enrichment strategy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Jones and 12 farmers are working to build up soil carbon by cultivating grasses that stay green all year round.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Like composting, the approach has already been proved experimentally.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Jones now hopes to show that it can be applied on working farms, and that the resulting carbon capture can be accurately measured.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's hoped in the future that projects such as these will demonstrate the role that farmers and other land managers can play in reducing the harmful effects of greenhouse gases.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, in countries like the United States, where most farming operations use large applications of fertilizer, changing such long standing habits will require a change of system.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Rattan Lal argues that farmers should receive payment, not just for the corn or beef they produce, but also for the carbon they can store in their soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another study being carried out.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: The use of soil to reduce carbon dioxide (CO₂) in the atmosphere
